items.title: Duplicate sensor readings in IoT monitoring
items.scenario: Two temperature sensors in the same room report nearly identical readings every minute because they measure the same air mass.
items.explanation: I(sensor A; sensor B) is high: knowing one reading sharply reduces uncertainty about the other. A feature-selection pipeline might keep only one sensor because the second adds little new information about the target fault label.

items.title: Zip code vs income in credit scoring
items.scenario: A model includes both zip code and a coarse income band. In many regions, zip code strongly predicts income band because neighborhoods cluster by socioeconomic level.
items.explanation: I(zip; income) can be large even though the relationship is not a simple linear correlation. Mutual information flags redundant features before training, reducing overfitting and compliance risk from proxy variables.

items.title: Token co-occurrence in language modeling
items.scenario: In English text, the token "San" is followed by "Francisco" far more often than independence would suggest.
items.explanation: Pointwise mutual information between "San" and "Francisco" is high at the bigram level; aggregated MI between context windows and next tokens motivates n-gram statistics and informs how much a model should rely on local context vs long-range dependencies.

items.title: Independent randomization in A/B tests
items.scenario: An experiment assigns users to variant A or B with a fair coin flip unrelated to any user attribute stored in the log.
items.explanation: I(assignment; user_age) = 0 and I(assignment; past_purchases) = 0 by design. Checking estimated MI near zero helps validate that randomization was implemented correctly before analyzing conversion rates.
